lede: Twenty weeks, build-first. From Python to a deployed, JVM-native capstone, then deep into evaluation, retrieval, fine-tuning math, inference economics, AI system design, and the portfolio polish that converts builds into offers. Every day: a focus, linked resources, and a hands-on task.

paragraphs: Budget ~10 hrs/week: ~2 hrs read/watch, ~6 hrs coding, ~2 hrs writing (a README or short post — this is where it snaps into your head). The single biggest predictor of retention is the build-to-theory ratio.
The pattern that works for engineers like you:

Two cadences: the build phase (Weeks 0–10) is hands-keyboard, capstone-driven — don't stall on theory. The depth phase (Weeks 11–18) is what makes you senior in AI, not just functional: it's anchored on Chip Huyen's AI Engineering (O'Reilly) — keep the book open as the spine for W4–16, then AI system design (W16) and interview prep (W20) close it out.
Don't fall for: tutorial hell, framework-collecting, chasing every model release. Pick one frontier API (Anthropic) + one open-source path (HuggingFace + Ollama) and stick to them.
bullets: Read the topic intro (15 min) — skim, don't deep-read.
Build the smallest thing that demonstrates the concept, with no framework. Highest-leverage step.
Rebuild with the framework — now you see what it was hiding.
Write a 200-word note: "what I'd use this for at work."

items: Editor: VS Code + Python/Jupyter extensions, or PyCharm if you want JetBrains-familiar.
API keys: Anthropic ($5 free), OpenAI ($5), Groq (free tier, fast inference for experiments).
Local models: install Ollama, pull llama3.2:3b and qwen2.5:7b.
Vector DB: start with Chroma (in-process, zero-ops). Move to pgvector/Qdrant later.
Observability: sign up for Langfuse (open-source, self-hostable).
Git: one repo ai-learning-lab, one folder per week.
Claude Code: install Claude Code now — Week 1 turns it into your daily driver.

timeSummary.deliverable: MLflow + Langfuse prompts + HF Hub + DVC + CI eval gate
Capstone on AWS, IaC, SaaS, eval-gated

paidCourses: AI Evals for Engineers & PMs (Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar) — highest-signal paid course in the space (~$2k). Pairs with Weeks 12 & 14.
Ed Donner — AI Engineer Core Track (~$15–20 on sale) — backbone for Weeks 2, 5, 10.
AI Engineer Agentic Track (~$15–20) — 8 projects across OpenAI SDK / CrewAI / LangGraph / AutoGen / MCP; drives Weeks 7–8.
AI Engineer Production Track (~$15–20) — AWS Bedrock/SageMaker, Lambda, Terraform, CI/CD, serverless multi-agent; drives Week 18.
notes.buyingTip: Buying tip: all three Ed Donner courses are on Udemy — the Personal Plan unlocks everything and beats buying individually; otherwise wait for the recurring ~$10–20 sale and never pay sticker. Chip Huyen's AI Engineering (the book you have) is the depth-weeks spine — no course replaces it.
pitfalls: You now own overlapping courses — don't binge them linearly. Use the resource map: one primary per week, the rest as reference. Watching all six Ed Donner courses + ByteByteGo + Cohen end-to-end is hundreds of hours and mostly redundant.
Don't let the depth weeks block shipping. The capstone (W15) is the hireable artifact; the later depth weeks deepen you but a portfolio project beats half-read theory. Ship first.
Don't chase "the correct framework." Week 8 is breadth on purpose — learn all four, commit to none.

Your backend rigor is a superpower past Week 7. Most AI engineers can't reason about throughput, idempotency, retries, or transactional semantics — your capstone and Week 17 should be 10× more production-ready than the median learner's.
Don't fine-tune unless evals say to. The #1 trap for technical learners.

Your edges are Weeks 3, 9, and 17. JVM-native AI + Temporal orchestration + inference optimization. Build the capstone on the JVM and write the blog posts — they're your differentiated interview assets.

weeks.title: Python Pre-Prep & Environment
weeks.goal: Goal: stop fighting Python. Be fluent enough that the language is invisible by Week 1. No AI yet — pure language + tooling delta from Kotlin.

days.focusSub: Syntax map, type hints, the traps

resources.label: Real Python — Python for Java devs

days.hands: Rewrite a Kotlin CSV transformer in Python with full type hints. Exercism Python: 3 starter exercises.

exercise.goal: Port a real Kotlin utility to idiomatic, fully-typed Python.
steps: Pick a small Kotlin/Java util you've written (e.g. a CSV transformer) and reimplement it in Python 3.12 with type hints on every signature.
Run mypy --strict and fix every error.
Do 3 starter exercises on the Exercism Python track to drill syntax.
acceptance: mypy --strict passes with zero errors.
The port handles the same edge cases as the original and is committed to week-00.
exercise.stretch: Add a pytest parametrized test covering 3 edge cases.

days.hands: Exercism: 4–5 exercises hitting comprehensions, dicts, generators.

exercise.goal: Internalise the Python idioms that replace Java boilerplate.
steps: Rewrite a data-holding class as a @dataclass(frozen=True) with type hints.
Convert one loop-and-append into a comprehension, and one into a generator.
Wrap a resource in a with context manager.
acceptance: A generator is used to stream rather than build a list.
4–5 Exercism exercises on comprehensions/dicts/generators pass.
exercise.stretch: Compare generator vs list memory with tracemalloc.

resources.label: Real Python — Async IO in Python

days.hands: Async httpx fetcher hitting 5 URLs concurrently; time it vs sequential.

exercise.goal: Get asyncio muscle memory and prove the speedup.
steps: Write an async httpx fetcher hitting 5 URLs with asyncio.gather.
Write the sequential version and time both.
Make one URL fail and ensure the batch still completes.
acceptance: The concurrent version is measurably faster than sequential.
A failing URL doesn't crash the whole batch; mypy is clean.
exercise.stretch: Add an asyncio.Semaphore(2) to cap concurrency and observe the timing change.

days.focusSub: Your structured-output validator all course long
days.hands: Model a billpay Payment as a Pydantic model with validators + a failing-validation test.

exercise.goal: Build the Pydantic structured-output validator you'll reuse all course.
steps: Model a billpay Payment as a Pydantic v2 model with a field validator and a model validator.
Write a test asserting a bad payload raises ValidationError.
Manage the project with uv + a committed lockfile.
acceptance: Valid payload parses; invalid raises a clear error.
uv.lock is committed.
exercise.stretch: Export the model's JSON Schema and adapt it into an LLM function/tool spec.

days.focusSub: Your Quarkus comfort zone, in Python
days.hands: FastAPI service: 2 endpoints (1 async) + 1 pytest. Commit to ai-learning-lab/week-00.

exercise.goal: Stand up your Python service comfort zone (FastAPI + pytest).
steps: Build a FastAPI app with two endpoints, one of them async.
Add Pydantic request/response models.
Write one pytest using TestClient.
acceptance: Both endpoints return the correct shapes.
pytest is green and the service is committed to week-00.
exercise.stretch: Add an exception handler that returns RFC-7807-style problem details.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: none — courses assume Python. Don't pay for syntax.
weeks.deliverable: Async FastAPI service + Pydantic models, typed, tested.

weeks.title: Claude Code & Agentic Coding
weeks.goal: Goal — front-loaded on purpose. You already use Claude Code daily; this week turns it into an autonomous coding system you drive efficiently, then pushes into multi-agent coding. Spine: Ed Donner — AI Coder. You'll use these skills to build every later project, so the payoff compounds.

days.hands: Write a real CLAUDE.md for your Billpay repo: build/lint/test commands, module map, conventions (Exposed, Temporal, KSP). Run one real task end-to-end.

exercise.goal: Turn CLAUDE.md into a real, working project constitution.
steps: Write a CLAUDE.md for your Billpay repo: build/lint/test commands, module map, conventions (Exposed, Temporal, KSP).
Run one real task end-to-end driven only by that file.
Trim anything Claude didn't actually use.
acceptance: Claude completes the task using only the documented commands/conventions.
The file is under ~1 page and committed.
exercise.stretch: Add an AGENTS.md pointer and confirm both stay in sync.

days.hands: Create 2 skills in .claude/skills/: one to generate Docusaurus pages from KSP variant metadata, one /pr-summary. Invoke both.

exercise.goal: Build reusable skills + slash commands for your workflow.
steps: Create two skills in .claude/skills/: one to generate Docusaurus pages from KSP variant metadata, one /pr-summary.
Invoke both on real inputs.
Document each skill's trigger and inputs.
acceptance: Both skills run and produce correct output on a real repo.
Each has a clear description so it triggers reliably.
exercise.stretch: Add a third skill that scaffolds a Temporal activity from a template.

days.focusSub: Run 100% of the time, unlike CLAUDE.md
days.hands: Add a PreToolUse hook that blocks force-push to main + scans writes for secrets (exit 2 = deny), and a PostToolUse hook running ktlint/detekt after edits. Verify they fire every time.

exercise.goal: Add deterministic guardrails with hooks.
steps: Add a PreToolUse or PostToolUse hook that runs your formatter/linter on edited files.
Add a hook that blocks commits to a protected branch.
Verify each fires on the relevant action.
acceptance: The format/lint hook runs 100% of the time on edits.
The protection hook actually blocks the disallowed action.
exercise.stretch: Wire a hook that runs the fast test subset before allowing a push.

resources.label: AI Coder — Wk3 (Agent Teams, Ralph Loops)

days.hands: Run 3 subagents concurrently to review auth, payments, migrations. Use plan mode for a multi-file refactor; try a Ralph Loop (persistent self-correcting agent) on a sandboxed task; roll back via checkpoint.

exercise.goal: Parallelise with subagents, Agent Teams, and checkpointed loops.
steps: Spawn 2+ subagents to work isolated parts of a task in parallel.
Run a Ralph-style loop with checkpoints on a multi-step refactor.
Capture where context isolation helped vs hurt.
acceptance: A task is completed by parallel subagents with merged output.
A checkpointed loop recovers from an intentional mid-run failure.
exercise.stretch: Measure wall-clock of parallel vs serial on the same task.

days.hands: Run claude -p headless in a GitHub Action / pre-commit. Wire an autonomous loop: raise a Jira issue → agent builds, tests, opens a PR. Connect a GitHub MCP server; skim the Agent SDK + Cowork.

exercise.goal: Drive Claude Code headless / in CI and via the Agent SDK.
steps: Run Claude Code headless on a scripted task (non-interactive).
Add a CI job that runs an autonomous PR-review pass.
Call the Agent SDK from a small script.
acceptance: The headless run produces the expected artifact with no manual input.
The CI job comments on a PR automatically.
exercise.stretch: Prototype an autonomous Jira→PR loop demo end-to-end.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: AI Coder (spine) + official docs.
weeks.deliverable: Billpay repo: CLAUDE.md + 2 skills + 2 hooks + CI PR-review + autonomous Jira→PR loop demo.

weeks.title: Gen AI Building Blocks
weeks.goal: Goal: understand how LLMs actually work, and ship 3 LLM-powered scripts from raw API calls — no framework.

days.hands: Note the $/M-token cost of 3 models you'll use. Sketch when each fits.

exercise.goal: Build cost/behaviour intuition for the models you'll use.
steps: Tabulate $/M input+output tokens for 3 models (frontier, mid, local).
Sketch when each model is the right pick.
Estimate the per-call cost of a 2k-in / 500-out request for each.
acceptance: A cost table covers 3 models with input+output pricing.
Each model has a one-line 'use when' rationale.
exercise.stretch: Add latency expectations (TTFT) per model from a quick probe.

days.hands: Tokenize 3 sample inputs; predict then verify token counts.

exercise.goal: Understand tokenization well enough to predict odd behaviour.
steps: Tokenize 3 sample inputs (code, prose, a non-English string) and predict counts before checking.
Find an example where whitespace/casing changes the token count.
Note the cost implication of your worst case.
acceptance: Predicted vs actual token counts are compared for 3 inputs.
You can explain one concrete 'why did the prompt behave oddly' case.
exercise.stretch: Compare two tokenizers (e.g. GPT-4o vs Llama) on the same input.

days.hands: Work notebooks ch.1–6. Build 1: CLI that summarizes a dir of markdown notes (raw anthropic SDK).

exercise.goal: Ship your first raw-API script with solid prompting.
steps: Work Anthropic's prompt-eng tutorial notebooks ch.1–6.
Build a CLI that summarizes a directory of markdown notes using the raw anthropic SDK.
Add a few-shot example and a chain-of-thought variant; compare outputs.
acceptance: The CLI summarizes real files from a folder.
Few-shot vs zero-shot difference is documented.
exercise.stretch: Add a --style flag that switches system prompts.

days.hands: Build 2: extractor — job description → {role, seniority, skills[], remote} as a Pydantic model.

exercise.goal: Get reliable structured output via function calling.
steps: Build an extractor: job description → {role, seniority, skills[], remote} as a Pydantic model.
Use the model's tool/function-calling to force the schema.
Feed 3 messy inputs and validate every result.
acceptance: All 3 inputs produce schema-valid objects.
A malformed model response is caught and retried or surfaced.
exercise.stretch: Add a confidence field and abstain when low.

days.hands: Build 3: LLM that can call 3 tools — get_weather, search_files, run_sql.

exercise.goal: Implement a multi-tool dispatch loop by hand.
steps: Give the LLM 3 tools — get_weather, search_files, run_sql.
Write the dispatch loop that routes tool calls and returns results.
Handle a tool error and a no-tool-needed turn.
acceptance: The model correctly selects and calls the right tool per query.
Tool errors are returned to the model rather than crashing.
exercise.stretch: Add parallel tool calls in a single turn and merge results.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: Core Track — Week 1
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-02-basics + README on tokens, context windows, cost math.

weeks.title: JVM-Native AI — Spring AI & LangChain4j
weeks.goal: Goal — your unfair advantage. Re-implement what you just learned in Python (LLM APIs, tool use, structured output) in your real stack — LangChain4j / Spring AI on Quarkus — so you can ship AI into Billpay without leaving the JVM. Almost no AI-engineering learner can do this; you already own the stack. On-ramp: Java Brains — From Java to AI (skim if any concept feels shaky).

days.focusSub: Spring AI vs LangChain4j vs Quarkus-LangChain4j
days.hands: Add quarkus-langchain4j to a Quarkus project; wire an Anthropic/OpenAI chat model; get your first completion from Kotlin.

exercise.goal: Pick your JVM AI stack deliberately.
steps: Spin up a Quarkus project with Quarkus-LangChain4j (and skim Spring AI).
Wire one chat call end-to-end.
Write a 1-pager: LangChain4j vs Spring AI vs Quarkus-LangChain4j for your use.
acceptance: A JVM chat call returns a completion.
The decision doc states which you'll use for the capstone and why.
exercise.stretch: Note the native-image implications for each option.

days.hands: Port your W2 summarizer to a LangChain4j AiServices interface; add a system prompt + temperature; stream the response.

exercise.goal: Use AiServices for typed chat, prompts, and streaming.
steps: Define an AiService interface with a prompt template.
Add parameters and stream tokens to the console.
Swap the model provider via config only.
acceptance: A typed AiService returns a completion from a template.
Streaming works and the provider is config-swappable.
exercise.stretch: Add a system message that enforces a persona and test it.

days.focusSub: map LLM output → Kotlin data classes
days.hands: Rebuild your W2 extractor to return a typed Kotlin data class directly — no manual JSON parsing.

exercise.goal: Map LLM output to Kotlin data classes safely.
steps: Have the AiService return a Kotlin data class (structured output).
Add validation for a required field.
Test a malformed-output path.
acceptance: LLM output deserializes into the data class.
A bad output is caught and handled, not propagated raw.
exercise.stretch: Generate the schema from the data class to constrain the model.

days.focusTitle: Tool calling & RAG on the JVM
days.hands: Expose 2–3 @Tool methods (incl. a read-only Oracle query); build a minimal RAG over Temporal docs using a pgvector embedding store.

exercise.goal: Do tool-calling + RAG natively on the JVM.
steps: Expose 2 @Tool methods the model can call.
Stand up a pgvector embedding store and ingest a small corpus.
Answer a question that requires retrieval + a tool call.
acceptance: The model calls a @Tool method and uses retrieved context.
Citations/sources are returned with the answer.
exercise.stretch: Add a hybrid (keyword + vector) retrieval path.

days.hands: Add chat memory + robust error handling (retry on malformed output, typed fallback) to your service. This is the pattern your capstone will ship on.

exercise.goal: Make the JVM LLM endpoint production-shaped.
steps: Add chat memory across turns.
Add retries + a fallback model on failure.
Validate/guard the output before returning.
acceptance: Multi-turn memory works within a session.
A forced provider failure falls back without erroring the caller.
exercise.stretch: Add a circuit breaker and surface it via a health endpoint.

weeks.udemy: Resources: Java Brains (on-ramp) + LangChain4j / Spring AI / Quarkus docs
weeks.deliverable: Repo billpay-ai-jvm — Quarkus/Kotlin service: tool-using, RAG-backed, typed, memory-aware LLM endpoint.

weeks.title: Foundation Model Internals
weeks.goal: Goal: understand why models behave as they do — the theory you skipped in Part I, now that you can contextualize it against systems you've built. Huyen Ch 2.

days.focusSub: temperature, top-k, top-p, greedy; structured outputs
days.hands: Implement temperature + top-p sampling over raw logprobs; show how each reshapes the output distribution.

exercise.goal: Make sampling parameters concrete by experiment.
steps: Generate completions at temperature 0, 0.7, 1.2 on the same prompt.
Sweep top-k and top-p and observe diversity vs coherence.
Force a structured output and find the sampling that keeps it valid.
acceptance: A short writeup ties each parameter to an observed effect.
You can state the sampling you'd use for deterministic extraction.
exercise.stretch: Reproduce a 'temperature too high breaks JSON' failure and fix it.

days.focusSub: CoT, best-of-N, ToT, parallel/sequential sampling, search-against-verifier
days.hands: Implement best-of-N, self-consistency, and a simple Tree-of-Thoughts + verifier search on a SQL/math task; chart accuracy vs token cost.

exercise.goal: Buy quality with test-time compute.
steps: Implement best-of-N sampling with a simple scorer.
Implement a tiny Tree-of-Thoughts or search-against-verifier loop.
Compare accuracy vs a single greedy call on a small task set.
acceptance: best-of-N beats single-shot on your task set.
A verifier/score gates the final answer.
exercise.stretch: Plot accuracy vs N and find the cost/quality knee.

resources.label: Huyen Ch 2 — The Probabilistic Nature of AI

days.hands: Run the same prompt 20× at temp 0 vs 1; quantify variance. Design a determinism strategy for billpay-grade reliability.

exercise.goal: Quantify the probabilistic nature + hallucination.
steps: Run the same prompt 10× and measure answer variance.
Construct a prompt that reliably hallucinates and one that doesn't.
Note which mitigations (grounding, lower temp) reduce it.
acceptance: Variance across runs is measured, not hand-waved.
A before/after shows a mitigation reducing hallucination.
exercise.stretch: Add self-consistency voting and measure the lift.

days.focusSub: SFT, RLHF, DPO; reward modeling (ORM/PRM), STaR, Meta-CoT
days.hands: Read a DPO preference dataset; diagram outcome- vs process-reward models (ORM/PRM) and how STaR bootstraps reasoning data. Sketch preference data for a billpay assistant.

exercise.goal: Trace the post-training pipeline that shapes behaviour.
steps: Write notes distinguishing SFT vs RLHF vs DPO.
Summarize reward modeling (ORM vs PRM) in your own words.
Map STaR / Meta-CoT to where reasoning ability comes from.
acceptance: A one-page note correctly separates the three post-training stages.
You can explain ORM vs PRM with an example.
exercise.stretch: Find a real model card and identify which stages it used.

days.focusSub: data corpora (Common Crawl/FineWeb), BPE, params, context, MoE
days.hands: Map the pre-training pipeline (corpora → cleaning → BPE → architecture). Build a decision table: per task class, which model size/family + why.

exercise.goal: Connect pre-training choices to model capability.
steps: Summarize data corpora (Common Crawl/FineWeb) and BPE's role.
Compute a rough params↔memory estimate for a given size.
Explain MoE vs dense in 3 bullets.
acceptance: A memory estimate for one model size is shown with the formula.
MoE tradeoffs are stated correctly.
exercise.stretch: Compare context-length scaling costs for two architectures.

days.focusSub: optional deep path — what's inside the weights

resources.label: Mike X Cohen — Deep LLM Mechanisms

days.hands: Optional: probe attention heads / activations on a small model. Pull the relevant module from Cohen's course — don't attempt all 90h; sample interpretability + attention internals.

exercise.goal: Take one peek inside the weights (optional).
steps: Run a small mechanistic-interpretability notebook (e.g. logit lens / an induction head demo).
Identify one interpretable behaviour.
Write 3 sentences on what you saw.
acceptance: A notebook runs and surfaces one interpretable signal.
You can describe it without jargon.
exercise.stretch: Probe a second behaviour and compare.

weeks.udemy: Spine: Huyen Ch 2 + ByteByteGo P1/P4 · Cohen (optional)
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-04-fm-internals — sampling + ToT/verifier eval + reward-model notes + determinism writeup.

weeks.title: Context Engineering & RAG
weeks.goal: Goal: build RAG from scratch (no framework), then rebuild with a framework. Corpus: Temporal docs — eat your own dog food. (Deep retrieval theory comes back in Week 6.)

days.hands: Write a 1-pager: 3 chunking strategies + when each wins.

exercise.goal: Choose chunking deliberately, not by default.
steps: Chunk one corpus 3 ways (fixed, recursive, semantic).
Embed each with all-MiniLM-L6-v2.
Eyeball retrieval quality on 3 queries per strategy.
acceptance: A 1-pager states 3 chunking strategies and when each wins.
You picked a default with a reason.
exercise.stretch: Add overlap and measure its effect on recall.

days.hands: Ingest Temporal docs; chunk 3 ways; embed with all-MiniLM-L6-v2.

exercise.goal: Build RAG end-to-end with no framework.
steps: Ingest a docs corpus into Chroma using the raw anthropic + chromadb + sentence-transformers.
Retrieve top-k and stuff context into the prompt.
Return answers with source chunks.
acceptance: A question is answered using retrieved context.
Sources are shown alongside the answer.
exercise.stretch: Add a 'no answer in context' guard.

days.hands: Add a re-ranker (cohere/rerank free or bge-reranker-v2-m3). Compare with/without.

exercise.goal: Add reranking to the retrieve→augment→generate loop.
steps: Add a reranker (cohere/rerank free tier or bge-reranker-v2-m3).
Compare answers with and without rerank on 5 queries.
Record where rerank changed the top result.
acceptance: A measurable quality difference with/without rerank is recorded.
The reranked pipeline is the new default.
exercise.stretch: Add MMR for diversity and compare.

days.hands: Rebuild in LlamaIndex/LangChain; implement hybrid + HyDE + query rewriting.

exercise.goal: Rebuild in a framework to see what it hides.
steps: Rebuild the pipeline in LlamaIndex or LangChain.
Add hybrid retrieval, HyDE, and query rewriting.
Diff the framework version against your scratch version.
acceptance: The framework version matches scratch behaviour.
HyDE + query rewriting are wired and tested.
exercise.stretch: Note which framework abstraction cost you control.

days.focusSub: pgvector swap · HNSW vs IVF · when to pick Qdrant/Weaviate/Pinecone

resources.label: Hamel — most RAG problems are retrieval problems
Qdrant benchmarks (vs pgvector / Weaviate / Pinecone / Milvus)

days.hands: Swap Chroma → pgvector; record recall@5 / MRR across chunking strategies in results.md. Then write a one-pager: HNSW vs IVF (when each wins on recall / latency / memory) + a decision matrix — Chroma / pgvector / Qdrant / Weaviate / Pinecone — scored on millions-of-vectors, metadata filtering, multi-tenancy, self-host vs SaaS.

exercise.goal: Make RAG production-shaped and pick a vector DB.
steps: Swap Chroma → pgvector; record recall@5 / MRR across chunking strategies in results.md.
Write the HNSW-vs-IVF tradeoff (recall/latency/memory).
Score Chroma/pgvector/Qdrant/Weaviate/Pinecone on a decision matrix.
acceptance: recall@5 / MRR numbers are recorded for pgvector.
A decision matrix justifies a vector-DB pick for millions of vectors + metadata filtering.
exercise.stretch: Load-test pgvector at 100k vectors and note p95 latency.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: Core Track — Week 5
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-05-rag — answers "what is sticky queue starvation?" with citations + numbers + vector-DB decision matrix.

weeks.title: Advanced RAG & Agents
weeks.goal: Goal: the retrieval and agent depth that actually moves production quality — beyond the basic pipeline you built in Week 5. Huyen Ch 6.

days.hands: Implement BM25 + dense + RRF fusion on your W5 corpus; compare recall across the three.

exercise.goal: Implement and compare the core retrieval algorithms.
steps: Implement BM25, dense, and RRF fusion over your W5 corpus.
Compare recall across the three.
Pick the winner for your data and justify it.
acceptance: recall is compared across BM25/dense/fusion.
A winner is chosen with numbers.
exercise.stretch: Add metadata pre-filtering and re-measure.

days.hands: Add a cross-encoder reranker + query rewriting; measure nDCG lift vs the W5 baseline.

exercise.goal: Optimise retrieval beyond the baseline.
steps: Add a cross-encoder reranker + query rewriting.
Add contextual retrieval (Anthropic-style).
Measure nDCG lift vs the W5 baseline.
acceptance: nDCG improves over baseline and the delta is recorded.
Each optimisation's individual contribution is noted.
exercise.stretch: Try contextual-retrieval prompt caching to cut cost.

days.hands: Add tabular retrieval over your Oracle schema — text-to-SQL plus row retrieval — feeding the agent.

exercise.goal: Retrieve beyond plain text.
steps: Add tabular retrieval over your Oracle schema (text-to-SQL + row retrieval).
Feed retrieved rows into the agent.
Test 3 questions that require structured data.
acceptance: The agent answers a question needing SQL/tabular retrieval.
Wrong-table/empty-result paths are handled.
exercise.stretch: Add a multimodal (image/table) retrieval example.

days.hands: Add a memory layer to your capstone agent (vector recall + rolling summary); test multi-session recall.

exercise.goal: Give your agent real memory.

specs.heading: The build — recommended pick (strongest for your portfolio)
specs.body: An agent that diagnoses a stuck Temporal workflow given a workflow ID, then proposes (and optionally executes, behind a human-approval signal) a mitigation. It sits exactly at the intersection of your strengths and AI engineering.
bullets: Input: a workflow ID (or an alert webhook).

Tools / MCP servers: query Temporal API (history, pending activities, stuck task queues); read Oracle business state via your Week-5 read-only MCP server; RAG over runbooks + Temporal docs (Week 5); a metrics tool (shard latency, sync-match rate).

Reasoning: a Quarkus-LangChain4j agent (or LangGraph if you'd rather stay in Python) classifies the failure (sticky-queue starvation, poller starvation, IO:XactSync contention, prepared-statement pinning, …) and drafts a mitigation.
Orchestration: the whole diagnosis runs as a Temporal workflow (Java SDK) — durable, retryable, with a human-approval Signal before any write action.
Quality gates: eval suite (Week 12) with golden incidents; LLM-as-judge on diagnosis correctness.
Observability: full Langfuse tracing of every tool call and token.

specs.heading: Alternative picks (if you want a change of domain)
bullets: Code Review Agent — reviews Kotlin PRs against your team style guide + anti-patterns; RAG over the guide + history.
India Relocation Concierge — multi-step planning (schools, neighborhoods, doc checklists). Personally useful, less technically flashy.
Wildlife Photography Assistant — RAG over EXIF + field guides; suggests lens/settings. Niche but distinctive.

Your JVM / Temporal / inference-opt edges become concrete answers here, not bullet points.

weeks.title: Inference Optimization
weeks.goal: Goal — YOUR SECOND EDGE. Distributed-systems thinking applied to LLM serving: throughput, batching, caching, latency-vs-cost. This is the strongest specialised material in the plan — most AI engineers can't reason about it; you do this for a living. Huyen Ch 9.

days.focusSub: TTFT, TPOT, throughput, goodput; GPU basics
days.hands: Benchmark TTFT / TPOT / throughput of your W10 model under load; build a latency-vs-cost table.

exercise.goal: Measure inference like a systems engineer.
steps: Benchmark TTFT / TPOT / throughput of your W10 model under load.
Define goodput for your SLO.
Build a latency-vs-cost table.
acceptance: TTFT/TPOT/throughput are measured under real load.
A latency-vs-cost table exists.
exercise.stretch: Plot throughput vs batch size and find the knee.

days.hands: Enable speculative decoding in vLLM; measure the speedup vs baseline.

exercise.goal: Optimise the model for inference.
steps: Enable speculative decoding in vLLM and measure speedup.
Quantize and re-measure.
Note any quality delta.
acceptance: Spec-decoding speedup vs baseline is measured.
Quality impact of quantization is recorded.
exercise.stretch: Try tensor parallelism across 2 GPUs (or simulate) and note scaling.

days.focusSub: continuous batching, KV cache, prefill/decode disaggregation
days.hands: Load-test vLLM continuous batching; chart throughput vs concurrency; observe KV-cache + prefill effects.

exercise.goal: Optimise the serving layer.

KV-cache/prefill behaviour is described.
exercise.stretch: Test prefill/decode disaggregation and compare.

days.focusSub: prompt cache, semantic cache, model gateway/router

Route easy queries to a small model via a gateway (LiteLLM).
Measure $ saved on a realistic traffic mix.
acceptance: Cache hit-rate and $ saved are measured.
Routing sends easy traffic to the cheap model correctly.
exercise.stretch: Add a cache-invalidation policy and test it.

days.hands: Design the serving tier for 500K throttled workflows: concurrency caps, batching, cache-hit targets — without scaling the r8g.8xlarge. This is your interview centerpiece.

exercise.goal: Design the serving tier for 500K throttled workflows.
steps: Design concurrency caps, batching, and cache-hit targets for the W9 fan-out.
Stay within the r8g.8xlarge Postgres budget.
Write it up as your interview centerpiece.
acceptance: A serving-tier design hits throughput targets within budget.
It ties explicitly to the Temporal fan-out.
exercise.stretch: Compute the $/1K-requests of the final design.

weeks.udemy: Spine: Huyen Ch 9 — no course matches your background here.
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-17-inference — load-test harness + spec-decoding bench + batching chart + gateway/cache + scale design doc.

weeks.title: AI Eng Architecture & Production Deployment
weeks.goal: Goal: assemble everything into a production reference architecture and deploy it to the cloud — the platform-engineering layer that is 70–80% of real production AI work. Huyen Ch 10 + Ed Donner Production Track.

weeks.title: Interview Prep, Portfolio & Job Search
weeks.goal: Goal — convert what you built into offers. Everything before this week made you a strong AI engineer; this week makes that legible to a hiring loop. STAR stories from the capstone + Temporal + inference work, take-home rehearsal, GitHub polish, blog posts as proof, target-company outreach. Hireable people skip this and leave money on the table.

days.focusSub: 5 stories that cover the loop — your edges first
days.hands: Write 5 STAR stories (Situation / Task / Action / Result, ≤300 words each): (1) Temporal fan-out at 500K from W9, (2) RAG quality lift from W5/W6, (3) eval-gated CI from W12/W13, (4) JVM-native AI from W3, (5) inference cost/latency win from W17. Each must end with a metric.

acceptance: 5 STAR stories exist, each ending in a metric.
